ID:       ddx-915240dd
Title:    routing: pick up agent endpoint-first redesign (upstream agent-0c6189f5)
Type:     task
Status:   in_progress
Priority: 1
Labels:   area:agent, area:routing, kind:integration, phase:build, workstream:agent-upgrade, blocked-on-upstream
Owner:    ddx
Desc:     Track the DDx-side pickup of the upstream endpoint-first routing redesign (agent-0c6189f5 in ~/Projects/agent tracker).

Until upstream ships the new endpoint-only config schema and runtime discovery, DDx's 32-test fake-migration children (ddx-68c372a6 through ddx-27e2b5ce) cannot drain on local models — the current named-profile 'vidar-omlx' vocabulary routes to dead endpoints and 404s on live ones (zero successes across a 30-attempt drain on 2026-04-21).

When agent-0c6189f5 lands:
1. Bump cli/go.mod to the new agent release.
2. Migrate .ddx/config.yaml from named profiles to endpoint blocks per the new schema.
3. Re-run 'ddx work --no-adaptive-min-tier --min-tier cheap --max-tier cheap' against the 32 ready fake-migration beads; expect them to resolve 'qwen3.6' → whatever live endpoint serves it and actually execute.
Accept:   DDx's config no longer names provider profiles in the agent.routing/agent.endpoints block (or equivalent post-redesign schema); drains against cheap-only routing land at live endpoints; 32 fake-migration children begin making progress on local models.

version: "1.0"
library:
    path: .ddx/plugins/ddx
    repository:
        url: https://github.com/easel/ddx-library
        branch: main
update_check:
    enabled: true
    frequency: 24h
agent:
    harness: claude
    endpoints:
        - type: lmstudio
          host: vidar
          port: 1234
          api_key: lmstudio
        - type: omlx
          host: vidar
          port: 1235
        - type: lmstudio
          host: bragi
          port: 1234
          api_key: lmstudio
        - type: lmstudio
          host: grendel
          port: 1234
          api_key: lmstudio
    routing:
        profile_ladders:
            default:
                - cheap
                - standard
                - smart
            cheap:
                - cheap
            fast:
                - fast
                - smart
            smart:
                - smart
        default_harness: agent
        model_overrides:
            cheap: qwen/qwen3.6
            fast: kimi/k2.5
            standard: codex/gpt-5.4
            smart: minimax/minimax-m2.7

package agent

import (
	"context"
	"fmt"
	"net/http"
	"net/http/httptest"
	"os"
	"path/filepath"
	"testing"

	agentlib "github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent"
	"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
	"github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
)

func TestNewServiceFromWorkDirUsesDDxEndpointConfigAndSkipsDeadEndpoint(t *testing.T) {
	t.Setenv("HOME", t.TempDir())

	dead := httptest.NewServer(http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, _ *http.Request) {
		http.Error(w, "bad gateway", http.StatusBadGateway)
	}))
	t.Cleanup(dead.Close)
	live := httptest.NewServer(http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
		require.Equal(t, "/v1/models", r.URL.Path)
		w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
		_, _ = w.Write([]byte(`{"data":[{"id":"Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit"}]}`))
	}))
	t.Cleanup(live.Close)

	workDir := t.TempDir()
	require.NoError(t, os.MkdirAll(filepath.Join(workDir, ".ddx"), 0o755))
	require.NoError(t, os.WriteFile(filepath.Join(workDir, ".ddx", "config.yaml"), []byte(fmt.Sprintf(`version: "1.0"
library:
  path: .ddx/plugins/ddx
  repository:
    url: https://github.com/easel/ddx-library
    branch: main
agent:
  endpoints:
    - type: lmstudio
      base_url: %s/v1
    - type: omlx
      base_url: %s/v1
`, dead.URL, live.URL)), 0o644))

	svc, err := NewServiceFromWorkDir(workDir)
	require.NoError(t, err)

	providers, err := svc.ListProviders(context.Background())
	require.NoError(t, err)
	require.Len(t, providers, 1)
	assert.Contains(t, providers[0].Name, "omlx")
	assert.NotContains(t, providers[0].Name, "lmstudio")

	dec, err := svc.ResolveRoute(context.Background(), agentlib.RouteRequest{
		Harness: "agent",
		Profile: "cheap",
		Model:   "qwen/qwen3.6",
	})
	require.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, providers[0].Name, dec.Provider)
	assert.Equal(t, "Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit", dec.Model)
}

cli/internal/agent/testdata/reviewer/bead-jsonl-oversized-line.jsonl:1:{"acceptance":"- [ ] JWT middleware installed in data-plane router as optional layer (runs when Authorization header present)
- [ ] JwtIssuer constructed at startup in serve.rs (dev: random key with warning)
- [ ] AuditAttribution populated on every audit write when ResolvedIdentity is present
- [ ] gRPC service.rs migrated from x-axon-database to composite x-axon-tenant-database
- [ ] gRPC service.rs gained optional JWT verify path
- [ ] cutover_jwt_test.rs integration test with real JWT end-to-end
- [ ] scripts/cutover-smoke.sh script runs end-to-end against dev server
- [ ] cargo test --workspace green
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean
- [ ] Legacy auth paths (--no-auth, --guest-role, Tailscale) still work unchanged","closing_commit_sha":"1a378fe785612ab9a24241977cf60c0c13e150b9","created_at":"2026-04-15T11:54:30.489788638Z","description":"Finish wiring the ADR-018 auth stack into the real request pipeline. After the smoke test on commit e83cf02, D3 already landed more than expected: `resolve_tenant_handler` composes `{tenant}:{database}` into the TenantRouter slug, giving real per-pair physical isolation. What remains is the three things that are *built but not installed*.

## What is already live (confirmed by smoke test)

- Path-based routing: /tenants/:t/databases/:d/... routed to per-pair SQLite files via composite slug
- GraphQL under the nested path
- Unprefixed REST + /graphql return 404
- HttpAxonClient in the SDK + the UI use the new paths
- Legacy X-Axon-Database header is gone from REST

## What is still NOT installed (the gap this bead closes)

### 1. JWT pipeline not enforced

B1 landed `auth_pipeline::jwt_verify_layer` + `JwtIssuer`. The real gateway router in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` does NOT install this layer. The server currently runs in legacy auth mode (Tailscale or --no-auth). Install the layer as an OPTIONAL middleware: if the request has an Authorization header, run the JWT pipeline; if not, fall back to legacy auth.

Wire point: in `build_router_with_auth` (or wherever the data-plane nested router is constructed), add `.layer(from_fn_with_state(state.clone(), jwt_verify_layer))` before the tenant_handler middleware. The layer needs access to a `JwtIssuer` and the `StorageAdapter`. Add them to the AppState struct.

Create the JwtIssuer at server startup in `serve.rs`. Read the signing key from an env var `AXON_JWT_KEY` (base64 HMAC secret); if unset, generate a random 32-byte key at startup and log a warning (dev mode). Production key management is a future concern.

The legacy auth path (crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs Identity + Role) still works for requests without an Authorization header — the JWT layer is a no-op pass-through when the header is absent. This preserves --no-auth, --guest-role, and Tailscale whois behavior exactly as today.

### 2. Audit attribution not populated

F1 added `AuditAttribution` as an optional field on `AuditEntry` plus the `with_attribution()` builder. No handler currently calls it. In the JWT middleware (step 1), after `ResolvedIdentity` is installed into the request extension, also expose a `current_attribution()` helper on AppState that the audit write sites can call.

Find every `AuditEntry::new(...)` call site in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` (there are probably 10-20) and chain `.with_attribution(current_attribution(&request))` when a ResolvedIdentity is present. When absent (legacy auth), leave the attribution as None — the existing `actor: String` field still works.

### 3. gRPC service.rs still reads x-axon-database

`crates/axon-server/src/service.rs:26` has `const AXON_DATABASE_HEADER: &str = "x-axon-database"` and `grpc_current_database` reads it from metadata. The gRPC service is entirely separate from the REST gateway and D3 did not touch it. Replace this header read with path-based extraction... except gRPC doesn't have URL paths in the same way. gRPC calls use service + method names, not paths. The database has to come from either:

(a) A metadata field (still a header-equivalent), OR
(b) A new field on every request proto

(a) is pragmatic. Rename the metadata header from `x-axon-database` to `x-axon-tenant-database` and carry a composite value `"{tenant}:{database}"`. This matches the REST composite slug. The gRPC service parses it and dispatches via the same `get_or_create_any` path.

Also add `x-axon-authorization` (or reuse the standard `authorization` metadata key) for JWT credentials. Run the JWT verify pipeline in the gRPC `check_auth` step before dispatching to the handler.

For this bead, the gRPC change is small:
- Rename the metadata constant
- Update `grpc_current_database` to parse the composite form
- Update the Rust + TS gRPC clients to send the composite form
- Add JWT auth path in `check_auth`

### 4. DatabaseRouter NOT swapped in (deferred — not needed right now)

D2 landed `DatabaseRouter` as a new primitive, but the gateway still uses `TenantRouter::get_or_create_any(slug)`. The smoke test confirmed this composite slug works correctly. Replacing TenantRouter with DatabaseRouter is a refactor that doesn't change behavior — defer it to a later cleanup bead. Not in this bead's scope.

## Scope summary

This bead does exactly three things:

1. Install B1's JWT middleware in the data-plane router, optional path (Authorization header present)
2. Populate F1's AuditAttribution at every audit write site when ResolvedIdentity is present
3. Migrate gRPC service.rs from x-axon-database header to the composite slug + optional JWT metadata

## Tests for this bead

### Integration test: crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs (NEW)

- create a JwtIssuer with a known key
- mint a JWT for a default user with grants on (default, default)
- start the in-process test server harness that gateway_test.rs uses
- send a request with Authorization: Bearer <jwt> to /tenants/default/databases/default/collections
- assert 200 (the JWT pipeline accepted + the handler saw ResolvedIdentity)

### Integration test: attribution populated

- send an entity-create request with a JWT
- query the audit log
- assert the audit entry has `attribution.user_id` set to the JWT sub

### gRPC test: composite slug

- send a CreateEntity gRPC request with metadata `x-axon-tenant-database: acme:orders`
- assert routing to the acme:orders database (check the storage file exists)

### Manual smoke test

Start the server, issue a JWT via `/control/tenants/default/credentials`, use the HttpAxonClient with the JWT, create an entity, verify the audit log has attribution. Write a short shell script under `scripts/cutover-smoke.sh` that does this end-to-end.

## Out of scope

- DatabaseRouter replacement — deferred
- OIDC federation — follow-up
- Signing key rotation — future ADR
- Removing legacy Identity/Role types from auth.rs — they still serve the --no-auth and Tailscale paths

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — install JWT middleware, thread AuditAttribution into audit writes (10-20 sites)
- crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs — construct JwtIssuer at startup
- crates/axon-server/src/service.rs — rename header constant, parse composite, add JWT path
- crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs — NEW
- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh — NEW
- Possibly sdk/typescript/src/client.ts — update gRPC metadata key if touching gRPC SDK surface

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical, just consume it
- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale path, out of scope for removal
- crates/axon-storage/* — no storage changes
- crates/axon-core/* — no type changes

## Verification

- cargo test -p axon-server (all prior tests green + new cutover_jwt_test)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

These tests exercise the full HTTP data-plane router through three lenses:\n+//!\n+//! 1. **Optional JWT layer (ADR-018 cutover)**: requests without an\n+//!    `Authorization` header fall through to the legacy no-auth path; requests\n+//! -x \"$AXON_BIN\" ]]; then\n+    fail \"Binary not found at $AXON_BIN after build\"\n+fi\n+\n+# ── Start server ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n+info \"Starting axon-server on HTTP :${AXON_HTTP_PORT} gRPC :${AXON_GRPC_PORT}…\"\n+AXON_JWT_KEY=\"${AXON_JWT_KEY}\" \\\n+AXON_NO_AUTH=true \\\n+AXON_HTTP_PORT=\"${AXON_HTTP_PORT}\" \\\n+AXON_GRPC_PORT=\"${AXON_GRPC_PORT}\" \\\n+AXON_SQLITE_PATH=\":memory:\" \\\n+\"${AXON_BIN}\" serve --storage memory &\n+SERVER_PID=$!\n+\n+cleanup() {\n+    kill \"${SERVER_PID}\" 2>/dev/null || true\n+    wait \"${SERVER_PID}\" 2>/dev/null || true\n+}\n+trap cleanup EXIT\n+\n+# Wait for the server to be ready.\n+for i in $(seq 1 20); do\n+    if curl -sf \"${BASE_URL}/health\" > /dev/null 2>&1; then\n+        info \"Server ready after ${i} attempts\"\n+        break\n+    fi\n+    sleep 0.3\n+    if [[ $i -eq 20 ]]; then\n+        fail \"Server did not become ready in time\"\n+    fi\n+done\n+\n+# ── Mint a JWT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n+# We need to produce a HS256 JWT. If python3 is available, use it; otherwise\n+# fall back to the /control/tenants/{id}/credentials endpoint (which requires\n+# a pre-provisioned tenant).\n+#\n+# Here we use a small python3 snippet to keep the script self-contained.\n+if ! command -v python3 &>/dev/null; then\n+    info \"python3 not found — skipping JWT tests (only running no-auth test)\"\n+    JWT_AVAILABLE=false\n+else\n+    JWT_AVAILABLE=true\n+    NOW=$(date +%s)\n+    EXP=$((NOW + 3600))\n+\n+    mint_token() {\n+        local grants_json=\"$1\"\n+        python3 - \"$AXON_JWT_KEY\" \"$NOW\" \"$EXP\" \"${TENANT}\" \"u-smoke-01\" \"$grants_json\" <<'PYEOF'\n+import sys, json, hmac, hashlib, base64\n+\n+secret = sys.argv[1].encode()\n+now = int(sys.argv[2])\n+exp = int(sys.argv[3])\n+aud = sys.argv[4]\n+sub = sys.argv[5]\n+grants = json.loads(sys.argv[6])\n+\n+def b64url(data: bytes) -> str:\n+    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(data).rstrip(b\"=\").decode()\n+\n+header = b64url(json.dumps({\"alg\":\"HS256\",\"typ\":\"JWT\"}).encode())\n+payload = b64url(json.dumps({\n+    \"iss\": \"axon-server\",\n+    \"sub\": sub,\n+    \"aud\": aud,\n+    \"jti\": \"smoke-jti-01\",\n+    \"iat\": now,\n+    \"nbf\": now,\n+    \"exp\": exp,\n+    \"grants\": grants,\n+}).encode())\n+\n+sig_input = f\"{header}.{payload}\".encode()\n+sig = b64url(hmac.new(secret, READ_GRANTS='{\"databases\":[{\"name\":\"smoke\",\"ops\":[\"read\"]}]}'\n+    WRITE_TOKEN=$(mint_token \"$WRITE_GRANTS\")\n+    READ_TOKEN=$(mint_token \"$READ_GRANTS\")\n+fi\n+\n+# ── Create the collection (needed before entity writes) ───────────────────────\n+info \"Creating collection ${COLLECTION} in ${TENANT}/${DATABASE}…\"\n+curl -sf -X POST \\\n+    \"${BASE_URL}/tenants/${TENANT}/databases/${DATABASE}/collections/${COLLECTION}\" \\\n+    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n+    -d '{}' > /dev/null\n+\n+# ── Test 1: No auth header → 201 \"${BASE_URL}/tenants/${TENANT}/databases/${DATABASE}/entities/${COLLECTION}/e-smoke-noauth\" \\\n+    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n+    -d '{\"data\":{\"x\":1}}')\n+[[ \"$STATUS\" == \"201\" ]] && pass \"No-auth entity create → ${STATUS}\" || fail \"Expected 201, got ${STATUS}\"\n+\n+# ── Test 2: Valid JWT write grant → 201 ───────────────────────────────────────\n+if [[ \\\n+        -d '{\"data\":{\"x\":2}}')\n+    [[ \"$STATUS\" == \"201\" ]] && pass \"JWT write → ${STATUS}\" || fail \"Expected 201, got ${STATUS}\"\n+else\n+    info \"Test 2: skipped (python3 not cutover-smoke.sh — end-to-end smoke test for the ADR-018 JWT cutover.\n+#\n+# Starts axon-server in --no-auth mode (no Tailscale required) with a\n+# known JWT secret, mints a token, and verifies:\n+#   1. No auth header → 201 (legacy path still works)\n+#   2. Valid JWT with write grant → 201\n+#   3. Valid JWT with read-only grant on POST → 403 op_not_granted\n+#   4. gRPC composite x-axon-tenant-database header is accepted\n+#\n+# Requirements: cargo, curl, jq, grpcurl (optional, for gRPC check).\n+#\n+# Usage:\n+#   ./scripts/cutover-smoke.sh\n+#\n+# Environment overrides:\n+#   AXON_HTTP_PORT   — HTTP port to use (default: 14170)\n+#   AXON_GRPC_PORT   — gRPC port to use (default: 14171)\n+#   AXON_JWT_KEY     — HMAC secret for JWT issuance (default: smoke-test-secret)\n+\n+set -euo pipefail\n+\n+AXON_HTTP_PORT=${AXON_HTTP_PORT:-14170}\n+AXON_GRPC_PORT=${AXON_GRPC_PORT:-14171}\n+AXON_JWT_KEY=${AXON_JWT_KEY:-smoke-test-secret-32bytes-xxxxx}\n+BASE_URL=\"http://127.0.0.1:${AXON_HTTP_PORT}\"\n+TENANT=\"acme\"\n+DATABASE=\"smoke\"\n+COLLECTION=\"items\"\n+\n+# ── Colour helpers ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n+RED='\\033[0;31m'\n+GREEN='\\033[0;32m'\n+YELLOW='\\033[1;33m'\n+NC='\\033[0m'\n+pass() { echo -e \"${GREEN}[PASS]${NC} $*\"; }\n+fail() { echo -e \"${RED}[FAIL]${NC} $*\"; exit 1; }\n+info() { echo -e \"${YELLOW}[INFO]${NC} $*\"; }\n+\n+# ── Build ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n+info \"Building axon-server…\"\n+cargo build -p axon-cli --quiet\n+\n+AXON_BIN=\"./target/debug/axon\"\n+if [[ ! -x \"$AXON_BIN\" ]]; then\n+    fail \"Binary not found at $AXON_BIN after build\"\n+fi\n+\n+# ── Start server ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n+info \"Starting axon-server on HTTP :${AXON_HTTP_PORT} gRPC :${AXON_GRPC_PORT}…\"\n+AXON_JWT_KEY=\"${AXON_JWT_KEY}\" \\\n+AXON_NO_AUTH=true \\\n+AXON_HTTP_PORT=\"${AXON_HTTP_PORT}\" \\\n+AXON_GRPC_PORT=\"${AXON_GRPC_PORT}\" \\\n+AXON_SQLITE_PATH=\":memory:\" \\\n+\"${AXON_BIN}\" serve --storage memory &\n+SERVER_PID=$!\n+\n+cleanup() {\n+    kill \"${SERVER_PID}\" 2>/dev/null || true\n+    wait \"${SERVER_PID}\" 2>/dev/null || true\n+}\n+trap cleanup EXIT\n+\n+# Wait for the server to be ready.\n+for i in $(seq 1 20); do\n+    if curl -sf \"${BASE_URL}/health\" > /dev/null 2>&1; then\n+        info \"Server ready after ${i} attempts\"\n+        break\n+    fi\n+    sleep 0.3\n+    if [[ $i -eq 20 ]]; then\n+        fail \"Server did not become ready in time\"\n+    fi\n+done\n+\n+# ── Mint a JWT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n+# We need to produce a HS256 JWT. If python3 is available, use it; otherwise\n+# fall back to the /control/tenants/{id}/credentials endpoint (which requires\n+# a pre-provisioned tenant).\n+#\n+# Here we use a small python3 snippet to keep the script self-contained.\n+if ! command -v python3 &>/dev/null; then\n+    info \"python3 not found — skipping JWT tests (only running no-auth test)\"\n+    JWT_AVAILABLE=false\n+else\n+    JWT_AVAILABLE=true\n+    NOW=$(date +%s)\n+    EXP=$((NOW + 3600))\n+\n+    mint_token() {\n+        local grants_json=\"$1\"\n+        python3 - \"$AXON_JWT_KEY\" \"$NOW\" \"$EXP\" \"${TENANT}\" sys.argv[5]\n+grants = json.loads(sys.argv[6])\n+\n+def b64url(data: bytes) -> str:\n+    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(data).rstrip(b\"=\").decode()\n+\n+header = b64url(json.dumps({\"alg\":\"HS256\",\"typ\":\"JWT\"}).encode())\n+payload = b64url(json.dumps({\n+    \"iss\": \"axon-server\",\n+    \"sub\": sub,\n+    \"aud\": aud,\n+    \"jti\": \"smoke-jti-01\",\n+    \"iat\": now,\n+    \"nbf\": now,\n+    \"exp\": exp,\n+    \"grants\": grants,\n+}).encode())\n+\n+sig_input = f\"{header}.{payload}\".encode()\n+sig = b64url(hmac.new(secret, sig_input, hashlib.sha256).digest())\n+print(f\"{header}.{payload}.{sig}\")\n+PYEOF\n+    }\n+\n+    WRITE_GRANTS='{\"databases\":[{\"name\":\"smoke\",\"ops\":[\"read\",\"write\"]}]}'\n+    READ_GRANTS='{\"databases\":[{\"name\":\"smoke\",\"ops\":[\"read\"]}]}'\n+    WRITE_TOKEN=$(mint_token \"$WRITE_GRANTS\")\n+    READ_TOKEN=$(mint_token \"$READ_GRANTS\")\n+fi\n+\n+# ── Create the collection (needed before entity writes) ───────────────────────\n+info \"Creating collection ${COLLECTION} in ${TENANT}/${DATABASE}…\"\n+curl -sf -X POST \\\n+    \"${BASE_URL}/tenants/${TENANT}/databases/${DATABASE}/collections/${COLLECTION}\" \\\n+    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n+    -d '{}' > /dev/null\n+\n+# ── Test 1: No auth header → 201 ─────────────────────────────────────────────\n+info \"Test 1: No auth header → legacy no-auth → 201\"\n+STATUS=$(curl -so /dev/null -w \"%{http_code}\" -X POST \\\n+    \"${BASE_URL}/tenants/${TENANT}/databases/${DATABASE}/entities/${COLLECTION}/e-smoke-noauth\" \\\n+    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n+    -d '{\"data\":{\"x\":1}}')\n+[[ \"$STATUS\" == \"201\" ]] && pass \"No-auth entity create → ${STATUS}\" || fail \"Expected 201, got ${STATUS}\"\n+\n+# ── Test 2: Valid JWT write grant → 201 ───────────────────────────────────────\n+if [[ \"$JWT_AVAILABLE\" == \"true\" ]]; then\n+    info \"Test 2: Valid JWT (write grant) → 201\"\n+    STATUS=$(curl -so /dev/null -w \"%{http_code}\" -X POST \\\n+        \"${BASE_URL}/tenants/${TENANT}/databases/${DATABASE}/entities/${COLLECTION}/e-smoke-jwt\" \\\n+        -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n+        -H \"Authorization: Bearer ${WRITE_TOKEN}\" \\\n+        -d '{\"data\":{\"x\":2}}')\n+    [[ \"$STATUS\" == \"201\" ]] && pass \"JWT write → ${STATUS}\" || fail \"Expected 201, got ${STATUS}\"\n+else\n+    info \"Test 2: skipped (python3 not available)\"\n+fi\n+\n+# ── Test 3: JWT read-only grant → 403 ─────────────────────────────────────────\n+if [[ \"$JWT_AVAILABLE\" == \"true\" ]]; then\n+    info \"Test 3: JWT read-only grant on POST → 403\"\n+    BODY=$(curl -s -X POST \\\n+        \"${BASE_URL}/tenants/${TENANT}/databases/${DATABASE}/entities/${COLLECTION}/e-smoke-ro\" \\\n+        -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n+        -H \"Authorization: Bearer ${READ_TOKEN}\" \\\n+        -d '{\"data\":{\"x\":3}}')\n+    CODE=$(echo \"$BODY\" | python3 -c \"import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); ${BODY}\"\n+else\n+    info \"Test 3: skipped (python3 not available)\"\n+fi\n+\n+# ── Test 4: gRPC composite header ─────────────────────────────────────────────\n+if command -v grpcurl &>/dev/null;  -d '{\"collection\":\"items\",\"id\":\"grpc-smoke-01\",\"data_json\":\"{\\\"x\\\":4}\",\"actor\":\"smoke\"}' \\\n+        \"127.0.0.1:${AXON_GRPC_PORT}\" axon.v1.AxonService/CreateEntity 2>&1)\n+   info \"Test 4: skipped (grpcurl not installed)\"\n+fi\n+\n+echo \"\"\n+pass \"All cutover smoke tests passed.\"","stderr":"","interrupted":false,"isImage":false,"noOutputExpected":false}}

data.text: The public module proxy has no tag past `v0.9.5`, so the only viable “new release” may be an untagged upstream commit or a local replace/pseudo-version. I’m checking the remote refs next before deciding whether this is a no-change upstream-blocked case or a real bump target.

The checked-in agent module `v0.9.5` already contains endpoint discovery and the endpoint-first public service shape; there is no newer published ref to bump to. I’m going to verify actual local routing and the ready fake-migration queue before touching code, because the remaining gate is evidence that cheap-only work reaches live endpoints.

data.output:
work is the primary operator-facing surface for draining the bead
execution queue. It is an alias for "ddx agent execute-loop" — all flags
and behavior are identical.

execute-loop is the primary queue-driven execution surface. It scans the
target project's execution-ready bead queue, claims the next ready bead,
runs "ddx agent execute-bead" on it from the project root, records the
structured result, and continues until no unattempted ready work remains.

Reach for execute-loop by default. Use "ddx agent execute-bead" directly
only as the primitive for debugging or re-running one specific bead.

Planning and document-only beads are valid execution targets — any bead
with unmet acceptance criteria and no blocking deps is eligible.

Close semantics (per execute-bead result status):
  success                      — close bead with session + commit evidence
  already_satisfied            — close bead (after repeated no_changes)
  no_changes                   — unclaim; may cooldown or close after retries
  land_conflict                — unclaim; result preserved under refs/ddx/iterations/
  post_run_check_failed        — unclaim; result preserved
  execution_failed             — unclaim
  structural_validation_failed — unclaim

Only success (and already_satisfied) closes the bead. Every other status
leaves the bead open and unclaimed so a later attempt can try again. Each
attempt is appended to the bead as an execute-bead event (status, detail,
base_rev, result_rev, preserve_ref, retry_after), and the underlying agent
session log is recorded under the execute-bead agent-log path.

By default execute-loop submits to the running ddx server as a background
worker and returns immediately. Use --local to run inline in the current
process.

Project targeting (multi-project servers):
  --project <path>    target a specific project root (absolute path or name)
  DDX_PROJECT_ROOT    env var fallback; used when --project is not set
  (default)           the git root of the current working directory

When submitting to a multi-project server you must ensure the target project
is registered with the server (run "ddx server" from that directory, or use
"ddx server projects register"). The server rejects unrecognised project paths.

Usage:
  ddx work [flags]

Examples:
  # Drain the current execution-ready queue once and exit
  ddx work

  # Pick one ready bead, execute it, and stop
  ddx work --profile default --once

  # Run continuously as a bounded queue worker
  ddx work --poll-interval 30s

  # Force a specific harness/model for a debugging pass
  ddx work --once --harness agent --model minimax/minimax-m2.7

  # Run inline in the current process
  ddx work --local --once

Flags:
      --adaptive-min-tier-window int   Trailing window size for adaptive min-tier evaluation (default 50)
      --effort string                  Effort level
      --from string                    Base git revision to start from (default: HEAD)
      --harness string                 Agent harness to use
  -h, --help                           help for work
      --json                           Output loop result as JSON
      --local                          Run inline in current process instead of server worker (default: submit to server)
      --max-cost float                 Stop the loop when accumulated billed cost exceeds USD; 0 = unlimited; subscription and local providers do not count (default 100)
      --max-tier string                Maximum tier for auto-escalation: cheap, standard, or smart (default: smart)
      --min-tier string                Minimum tier for auto-escalation: cheap, standard, or smart (default: cheap)
      --model string                   Model override
      --model-ref string               Model catalog reference (e.g. code-medium); resolved via the model catalog
      --no-adaptive-min-tier           Disable adaptive min-tier promotion based on trailing cheap-tier success rate
      --no-review                      Skip post-merge review (e.g. for doc-only beads or tight iteration loops)
      --once                           Process at most one ready bead
      --poll-interval duration         Poll interval for continuous scanning; zero drains current ready work and exits
      --profile string                 Routing profile: default, cheap, fast, or smart (default "default")
      --project string                 Target project root path or name (default: CWD git root). Env: DDX_PROJECT_ROOT
      --provider string                Provider name (e.g. vidar, openrouter); selects a named provider from config
      --review-harness string          Harness to use for the post-merge reviewer (default: same as implementation harness)
      --review-model string            Model override for the post-merge reviewer (default: smart tier)

Global Flags:
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output

List beads whose dependencies are all closed, sorted by priority.

By default this is the dependency-ready set: any open bead with no blocking
deps, including epics and beads on retry cooldown. 'ddx work' operates on a
narrower "execution-ready" subset — use --execution to see exactly what
'ddx work' would pick from, or the reverse: if 'ddx bead ready' shows work
but 'ddx work' reports none, the diff is epics, cooldown-waiting beads,
beads with execution-eligible=false, and superseded beads.

Usage:
  ddx bead ready [flags]

Flags:
      --execution   Filter to the execution-ready subset (what ddx work picks from): open, deps-closed, not an epic, execution-eligible, not superseded, not on retry cooldown
  -h, --help        help for ready
      --json        Output as JSON

Global Flags:
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output

ID:       ddx-68c372a6
Title:    test: migrate TestExecuteBeadMerge to real git + script harness
Type:     task
Status:   open
Priority: 3
Labels:   phase:test, area:cli, area:agent, kind:refactor, workstream:fake-migration
Parent:   ddx-d9df348d
Deps:     ddx-915240dd
Desc:     Migrate cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go TestExecuteBeadMerge() from fakeExecuteBeadGit + fakeAgentRunner to real git + script harness.

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptHarnessRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.
- Replace fakeExecuteBeadGit with the RealLandingGitOps flow used in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go.
- Replace fakeAgentRunner with the script harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go) driven by a per-attempt directive file. When the test needs to simulate 'agent emits no commits' or 'agent emits a dirty worktree', encode that as script directives rather than a canned Result struct.
- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadMerge$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.
Accept:   1) TestExecuteBeadMerge no longer references fakeExecuteBeadGit or fakeAgentRunner. 2) Test dispatches through RealLandingGitOps + newScriptHarnessRepo (or equivalent real-git harness). 3) cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadMerge$' -count=1 green. 4) No other test in agent_execute_bead_test.go is modified.

data.output:
ID:       agent-0c6189f5
Title:    redesign: endpoint-first routing (kill named-provider profiles)
Type:     task
Status:   closed
Priority: 0
Labels:   area:routing, area:providers, kind:design, kind:refactor, phase:design
Owner:    erik
Desc:     User-stated design direction (2026-04-21, DDx session). Current routing is built around named provider profiles (e.g. 'vidar-omlx', 'vidar-coder', 'bragi') each with a hard-coded model declaration. This breaks in multiple ugly ways and drove a zero-success drain of 32 test-migration beads on DDx:

Failure 1: 'cheap: qwen/qwen3.6' in .ddx/config.yaml routed to a DEAD vidar lmstudio endpoint (http://vidar:1234, 502 Bad Gateway) because the named profile declared its model as 'qwen3.5-27b' and the request fuzzy-matched on that string before any liveness check ran.

Failure 2: When failover hit vidar-omlx (http://vidar:1235), the request carried 'qwen3.5-27b' as the model string — but vidar-omlx actually serves 'Qwen3.5-27B-4bit' and 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit'. Every attempt 404'd with 'Model not found'.

Failure 3: routing_smart.go already has NormalizeModelID (internal/provider/openai/discovery.go) doing case-insensitive + suffix match against a discovered catalog, but it is only wired in cmd/agent routing_smart.go — NOT in the Service.Execute path that every consumer (DDx, etc.) actually hits.

Desired shape:

1. Config = endpoint pairs, not named profiles:
   endpoints:
     - {type: lmstudio, host: vidar, port: 1234}
     - {type: omlx, host: vidar, port: 1235}
     - {type: lmstudio, host: bragi, port: 1234}
     - {type: openrouter, api_key: ...}

2. On every dispatch, probe each endpoint's /v1/models with a short timeout. Skip endpoints that 502/timeout/empty.

3. Build a live {endpoint, model_id} matrix from the probe. The model_id is whatever the server actually serves — no per-provider name hardcoding.

4. Resolve the request (user asks for 'qwen3.6' or any case-variant) against the matrix:
   - case-insensitive contains / suffix / prefix match against every live model_id
   - if multiple endpoints match, tie-break on prior performance (latency, success rate, cost) from the routing-metrics store
   - if one matches, use it
   - if none match, explicit error ('no live endpoint offers a match for qwen3.6') + caller escalates

5. Kill the named-profile config-block lookups in the routing hot path. User-facing aliases (labels) are fine as shorthand but resolve through the same discovery flow.

6. NormalizeModelID's logic is the right primitive; extend and wire it into Service.Execute so every consumer benefits.
Accept:   (1) Config schema supports endpoint-only blocks (no per-provider 'name' or 'model' fields required). (2) Service.Execute (not just cmd/agent routing_smart.go) probes /v1/models and normalizes the requested model against live catalogs case-insensitively, including suffix/prefix variants (Qwen3.5-27B-4bit ↔ qwen3.5-27b, qwen/qwen3.6 ↔ Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit). (3) Dead endpoints are skipped at probe time, not on the failing request. (4) When multiple live endpoints serve the same requested model, selection uses routing-metrics signals, not config ordering. (5) DDx's .ddx/config.yaml can express 'give me the best qwen3.6 you can find' without naming vidar-omlx or any specific provider. (6) Integration test: with two endpoints A (dead) and B (live, serves Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit), 'qwen3.6' resolves to B without hand-holding. (7) Regression: no existing acceptance test regresses.

docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-18-ddx-agent-service-interface.md:481:**Decisive C-favoring argument: comparison suite.** A stated project goal is that ddx-agent's native harness should *outperform* the subprocess wrappers (claude/codex/opencode) on batch noninteractive tasks. Proving that requires a comparison suite that runs identical prompts through every harness implementation and measures latency, cost, success rate, output quality. Today that suite is split: harness invocation lives in DDx (`cli/internal/agent/compare.go`, `cli/internal/agent/benchmark.go`), routing lives in DDx, but the native agent runtime lives in ddx-agent. Under C, the comparison suite collapses into one module that can A/B test (`harness=claude` subprocess) vs (`harness=agent-native`, `provider=bragi`, `model=qwen3.6`) with the same input/output schema and metrics collection. Under A or B, the suite still has to bridge two modules and measurement parity is harder to maintain.

.ddx/executions/20260422T233901-96b38324/prompt.md:5:Track the DDx-side pickup of the upstream endpoint-first routing redesign (agent-0c6189f5 in ~/Projects/agent tracker).

Until upstream ships the new endpoint-only config schema and runtime discovery, DDx's 32-test fake-migration children (ddx-68c372a6 through ddx-27e2b5ce) cannot drain on local models — the current named-profile 'vidar-omlx' vocabulary routes to dead endpoints and 404s on live ones (zero successes across a 30-attempt drain on 2026-04-21).

When agent-0c6189f5 lands:
1. Bump cli/go.mod to the new agent release.
2. Migrate .ddx/config.yaml from named profiles to endpoint blocks per the new schema.
3. Re-run 'ddx work --no-adaptive-min-tier --min-tier cheap --max-tier cheap' against the 32 ready fake-migration beads; expect them to resolve 'qwen3.6' → whatever live endpoint serves it and actually execute.

.ddx/executions/20260422T205855-ce3545af/prompt.md:5:Track the DDx-side pickup of the upstream endpoint-first routing redesign (agent-0c6189f5 in ~/Projects/agent tracker).

Until upstream ships the new endpoint-only config schema and runtime discovery, DDx's 32-test fake-migration children (ddx-68c372a6 through ddx-27e2b5ce) cannot drain on local models — the current named-profile 'vidar-omlx' vocabulary routes to dead endpoints and 404s on live ones (zero successes across a 30-attempt drain on 2026-04-21).

When agent-0c6189f5 lands:
1. Bump cli/go.mod to the new agent release.
2. Migrate .ddx/config.yaml from named profiles to endpoint blocks per the new schema.
3. Re-run 'ddx work --no-adaptive-min-tier --min-tier cheap --max-tier cheap' against the 32 ready fake-migration beads; expect them to resolve 'qwen3.6' → whatever live endpoint serves it and actually execute.

.ddx/executions/20260418T064756-b495509a/prompt.md:484:+    "description": "## Problem\n\nNo single document traces the provider/model/harness resolution path from CLI flags to the final HTTP request. SD-015 and SD-023 are SPECS — they describe what should happen in 5 routing modes. The code lives across routing.go, discovery.go, agent_runner.go, runner.go, and agent_cmd.go. A newcomer debugging a routing bug must infer the flow by reading all of them.\n\nThe audit for epic ddx-2d974641 turned up a RouteRequest struct missing its Provider field, and a parallel RunOptions.Provider path that bypasses routing — neither of which is documented. This is the kind of hidden structural defect a Resolution Path document would have flagged on first read.\n\n## Fix\n\nWrite docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (or similar location; coordinate with SD-015's author). The document traces one concrete invocation end-to-end:\n\n    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6\n\nWalk through every layer, with file:line citations:\n\n1. **CLI parse** — cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go flag extraction (line references).\n\n2. **RouteFlags -\u003e RouteRequest normalization** — routing.go:NormalizeRouteRequest. What each CLI flag maps to; which flags are discarded when they don't match an expected shape; the current gap around --provider.\n\n3. **Discovery probe** — discovery.go:DiscoverProviderModels. When it fires; what it caches; the 30s TTL; how failures are handled.\n\n4. **Candidate planning** — routing.go:BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate. For each registered harness, the state map lookup and the viability checks.\n\n5. **Fuzzy matching** — discovery.go:FuzzyMatchModel. Pool selection (global vs per-provider), normalization (current gaps), prefix + tiebreak algorithm.\n\n6. **Scoring** — routing.go:scoreCandidate. Per-profile scoring, cost class, historical success rate, provider-affinity bonus (when implemented).\n\n7. **Candidate ranking** — routing.go:RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate. Non-viable rejection, stable tiebreak.\n\n8. **Dispatch** — runner.go:Run. Harness-specific branches (virtual, agent, script, HTTP-provider-embedded, default exec). For embedded: agent_runner.go:RunAgent -\u003e resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider -\u003e agent library call.\n\n9. **Wire-level request** — ddx-agent builds the HTTP body (system_prompt + messages + tools), sends to the provider. What the server receives.\n\n10. **Response parsing** — streaming or non-streaming decode; where 'unexpected end of JSON input' (ddx-6a5dfe35) surfaces.\n\nInclude a SEQUENCE DIAGRAM (mermaid or ASCII) showing the layers.\n\nInclude a GAP TABLE at the top listing every currently-missing piece (as of whatever the SD-015 implementation state is) with links to the specific beads tracking each gap.\n\nKeep the document MAINTAINED alongside the code: update it when RouteRequest gains a field, when a new routing mode lands, when fuzzy match gains a normalization step.\n\n## Files likely touched\n\n- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (new)\n- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md — add a single-line pointer at the top of the 'Overview' section\n- skills/ddx/reference/agents.md — add a one-line pointer in 'Harness / profile / persona dispatch' section so agents consulting the skill can follow up to the full trace when needed\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Rewriting SD-015 or SD-023 — this doc complements them; they describe intent, this describes current reality.\n- Documenting future routing modes that haven't landed.",

.ddx/executions/20260421T163240-eb3cb31f/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Target UX\n\nThe user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:\n\n### default profile (the common case)\nTry local models first, escalate to cloud if:\n- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt\n- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)\n- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)\n- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)\n\nEscalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.\n\n### cheap profile\nLocal only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.\n\n### fast profile\nCloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.\n\n### smart profile\nCloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.\n\n## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)\n\nAs of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).\n\n## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)\n\nThis epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:\n\n- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation\n- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics\n- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead\n\nWith those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.\n\n## Scope\n\n### Config schema additions\n\n.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:\n\n    agent:\n      routing:\n        profile_ladders:\n          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate\n          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only\n          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart\n          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation\n        model_overrides:\n          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6\n          fast:     kimi/k2.5\n          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality\n          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7\n\nCurrent profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.\n\n### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)\n\nFor a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:\n- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).\n- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.\n- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.\n\n### User-facing CLI\n\n- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.\n- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.\n- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).\n\n### Telemetry + measurement\n\nEvery execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.\n\n## Exit gate\n\n'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:\n- Claims the bead.\n- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.\n- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.\n- If standard fails, retries at smart.\n- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.\n- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.\n- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.\n\n## Metadata\n\nGoverning spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.",
.ddx/executions/20260421T163240-eb3cb31f/prompt.md:5:## Target UX

The user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:

### default profile (the common case)
Try local models first, escalate to cloud if:
- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt
- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)
- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)
- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)

Escalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.

### cheap profile
Local only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.

### fast profile
Cloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.

### smart profile
Cloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.

## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)

As of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).

## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)

This epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:

- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation
- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs
- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs
- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics
- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead

With those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.

## Scope

### Config schema additions

.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:

    agent:
      routing:
        profile_ladders:
          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate
          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only
          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart
          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation
        model_overrides:
          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6
          fast:     kimi/k2.5
          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality
          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7

Current profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.

### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)

For a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:
- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).
- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.
- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.

### User-facing CLI

- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.
- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.
- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).

### Telemetry + measurement

Every execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.

## Exit gate

'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:
- Claims the bead.
- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.
- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.
- If standard fails, retries at smart.
- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).

## Out of scope

- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.
- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.
- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.

## Metadata

Governing spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.

.ddx/executions/20260418T212635-79a847ad/prompt.md:5:## Problem

The fuzzy matcher at cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:62-108 has two defects that compound:

### Defect 1: strict case-sensitive prefix with no canonical-prefix stripping

fuzzyMatchInPool (discovery.go:76) does literal case-sensitive prefix check:

    for _, m := range pool {
        if len(m) > len(input) && m[:len(input)] == input {
            candidates = append(candidates, candidate{...})
        }
    }

Cannot bridge 'qwen/qwen3.6' (canonical ref) -> 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit' (vidar-omlx native). Case mismatch + slash-prefix mismatch at first byte.

### Defect 2: matcher collapses provider identity

FuzzyMatchModel (discovery.go:62-71) pools d.AllModels() — a deduplicated union across EVERY discovered provider. Callers then do a second lookup via ProvidersForModel(match) to pick a provider, but that lookup is not gated on --provider preference. A model name appearing on any provider can leak routing to that provider even under a --provider pin.

These two defects must be fixed together. Per Opus fresh-eyes review: per-provider-pool without normalization matches even less than the current union pool. Splitting them creates a merge-conflict hazard (same file, same test surface, one coherent change).

## Fix

Update the discovery.go fuzzy-match layer as one coherent change:

### 1. Normalize input and pool entries

Add canonicalization helper:

    func canonicalizeModelKey(s string) string {
        // Strip exactly ONE leading 'vendor/' segment if present (preserve
        // path structure for future multi-segment refs — do NOT strip all
        // slashes blindly).
        if slash := strings.Index(s, '/'); slash > 0 {
            s = s[slash+1:]
        }
        return strings.ToLower(s)
    }

Per codex fresh-eyes review: ONLY the leading vendor namespace is stripped. 'openai/gpt-4' -> 'gpt-4'. 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6' -> 'claude-sonnet-4-6'. 'qwen/qwen3.6' -> 'qwen3.6'. A hypothetical 'a/b/c' becomes 'b/c' (not 'c').

### 2. Case-insensitive prefix on canonicalized forms

Apply prefix matching on canonicalized(input) vs canonicalized(pool entry). Shortest-suffix tiebreak remains. Return the ORIGINAL pool entry (preserve casing) when a match is found.

Negative case: 'minimax/minimax-m2.7' vs 'MiniMax-M2.5-MLX-4bit' normalizes to 'minimax-m2.7' vs 'minimax-m2.5-mlx-4bit'. Prefix check: 'minimax-m2.5-mlx-4bit' does NOT start with 'minimax-m2.7' (divergence at '.5' vs '.7'). Returns no match. CORRECT — m2.7 is not m2.5.

### 3. Per-provider variant

Add DiscoveryResult.FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider(input, providerName) that pools ONLY the named provider's models. Exact-match + fuzzy-match semantics identical to the global version, running against the narrower pool.

    func (d *DiscoveryResult) FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider(input, providerName string) (model, provider string, fuzzy bool)

Returns (resolvedModelID, providerName, isFuzzy) tuple. Provider identity is preserved so downstream scoring doesn't need a second lookup.

### 4. Callers

Keep the existing AllModels() path (useful for unpinned routing). Callers with a provider pin should use the per-provider variant first and fall back to global discovery only if the pinned provider has no match (SD-015 'soft preference' semantics: try pinned first, then broader).

### 5. Exact-match precedence

Preserve exact-match precedence (both case-sensitive and case-insensitive). Exact > fuzzy.

### 6. Known tiebreak quirk (track, don't fix here)

Per Opus review: 'qwen3' matches both 'qwen3.5-27b-4bit' and 'qwen3.6-35b-a3b-4bit'; shortest-suffix picks qwen3.5. A user asking for 'qwen3' probably wants the latest. This is inherited behavior from the existing tiebreak rule and is out of scope for THIS bead — add a follow-up bead and a test documenting the current (possibly-wrong) resolution so a future change is a conscious policy choice.

## Files likely touched

- cli/internal/agent/discovery.go — canonicalizeModelKey, updated fuzzyMatchInPool, new FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider
- cli/internal/agent/discovery_test.go — new cases: vendor-prefix stripping, case folding, exact-match precedence, ambiguity tiebreak, negative (version divergence), per-provider pool match, pinned-provider fallback behavior
- New follow-up bead tracking 'prefer latest version on ambiguous tiebreak' (file in the same commit so the link is durable)

## Depends on

None; self-contained discovery-layer change. Unblocks ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/profile discovery integration) and ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier-affinity) once ddx-8610020e (Provider field) is also in place.
.ddx/executions/20260418T212635-79a847ad/prompt.md:8:1. fuzzyMatchInPool returns the correct match for case + vendor-prefix variants: fuzzy('qwen/qwen3.6', ['Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', 'Qwen3.5-27B-4bit']) -> ('Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', true).

2. Negative case: fuzzy('minimax/minimax-m2.7', ['MiniMax-M2.5-MLX-4bit']) -> ('', false). Version-patch divergence does not force a match.

3. Exact match still takes precedence: fuzzy('Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', ['Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit-v2']) -> ('Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', false).

4. Shortest-suffix tiebreak is unchanged for already-matching cases.

5. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/ -run Fuzzy passes, plus at least 5 new table-driven cases covering the normalization paths.

.ddx/executions/20260418T021346-948dfcde/prompt.md:3:    <title>routing: fuzzy matcher lacks case + canonical-prefix normalization — qwen/qwen3.6 can't match Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit</title>
.ddx/executions/20260418T021346-948dfcde/prompt.md:5:## Problem

The fuzzy matcher at cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:62-108 has two defects that compound:

### Defect 1: strict case-sensitive prefix with no canonical-prefix stripping

fuzzyMatchInPool (discovery.go:76) does literal case-sensitive prefix check:

    for _, m := range pool {
        if len(m) > len(input) && m[:len(input)] == input {
            candidates = append(candidates, candidate{...})
        }
    }

Cannot bridge 'qwen/qwen3.6' (canonical ref) -> 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit' (vidar-omlx native). Case mismatch + slash-prefix mismatch at first byte.

### Defect 2: matcher collapses provider identity

FuzzyMatchModel (discovery.go:62-71) pools d.AllModels() — a deduplicated union across EVERY discovered provider. Callers then do a second lookup via ProvidersForModel(match) to pick a provider, but that lookup is not gated on --provider preference. A model name appearing on any provider can leak routing to that provider even under a --provider pin.

These two defects must be fixed together. Per Opus fresh-eyes review: per-provider-pool without normalization matches even less than the current union pool. Splitting them creates a merge-conflict hazard (same file, same test surface, one coherent change).

## Fix

Update the discovery.go fuzzy-match layer as one coherent change:

### 1. Normalize input and pool entries

Add canonicalization helper:

    func canonicalizeModelKey(s string) string {
        // Strip exactly ONE leading 'vendor/' segment if present (preserve
        // path structure for future multi-segment refs — do NOT strip all
        // slashes blindly).
        if slash := strings.Index(s, '/'); slash > 0 {
            s = s[slash+1:]
        }
        return strings.ToLower(s)
    }

Per codex fresh-eyes review: ONLY the leading vendor namespace is stripped. 'openai/gpt-4' -> 'gpt-4'. 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6' -> 'claude-sonnet-4-6'. 'qwen/qwen3.6' -> 'qwen3.6'. A hypothetical 'a/b/c' becomes 'b/c' (not 'c').

### 2. Case-insensitive prefix on canonicalized forms

Apply prefix matching on canonicalized(input) vs canonicalized(pool entry). Shortest-suffix tiebreak remains. Return the ORIGINAL pool entry (preserve casing) when a match is found.

Negative case: 'minimax/minimax-m2.7' vs 'MiniMax-M2.5-MLX-4bit' normalizes to 'minimax-m2.7' vs 'minimax-m2.5-mlx-4bit'. Prefix check: 'minimax-m2.5-mlx-4bit' does NOT start with 'minimax-m2.7' (divergence at '.5' vs '.7'). Returns no match. CORRECT — m2.7 is not m2.5.

### 3. Per-provider variant

Add DiscoveryResult.FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider(input, providerName) that pools ONLY the named provider's models. Exact-match + fuzzy-match semantics identical to the global version, running against the narrower pool.

    func (d *DiscoveryResult) FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider(input, providerName string) (model, provider string, fuzzy bool)

Returns (resolvedModelID, providerName, isFuzzy) tuple. Provider identity is preserved so downstream scoring doesn't need a second lookup.

### 4. Callers

Keep the existing AllModels() path (useful for unpinned routing). Callers with a provider pin should use the per-provider variant first and fall back to global discovery only if the pinned provider has no match (SD-015 'soft preference' semantics: try pinned first, then broader).

### 5. Exact-match precedence

Preserve exact-match precedence (both case-sensitive and case-insensitive). Exact > fuzzy.

### 6. Known tiebreak quirk (track, don't fix here)

Per Opus review: 'qwen3' matches both 'qwen3.5-27b-4bit' and 'qwen3.6-35b-a3b-4bit'; shortest-suffix picks qwen3.5. A user asking for 'qwen3' probably wants the latest. This is inherited behavior from the existing tiebreak rule and is out of scope for THIS bead — add a follow-up bead and a test documenting the current (possibly-wrong) resolution so a future change is a conscious policy choice.

## Files likely touched

- cli/internal/agent/discovery.go — canonicalizeModelKey, updated fuzzyMatchInPool, new FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider
- cli/internal/agent/discovery_test.go — new cases: vendor-prefix stripping, case folding, exact-match precedence, ambiguity tiebreak, negative (version divergence), per-provider pool match, pinned-provider fallback behavior
- New follow-up bead tracking 'prefer latest version on ambiguous tiebreak' (file in the same commit so the link is durable)

## Depends on

None; self-contained discovery-layer change. Unblocks ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/profile discovery integration) and ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier-affinity) once ddx-8610020e (Provider field) is also in place.

2. Negative case: fuzzy('minimax/minimax-m2.7', ['MiniMax-M2.5-MLX-4bit']) -> ('', false). Version-patch divergence does not force a match.

3. Exact match still takes precedence: fuzzy('Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', ['Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit-v2']) -> ('Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', false).

This is the kind of hidden structural defect a Resolution Path document would have flagged on first read.\n\n## Fix\n\nWrite docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (or similar location; coordinate with SD-015's author). The document traces one concrete invocation end-to-end:\n\n    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6\n\nWalk through every layer, with file:line citations:\n\n1. **CLI parse** — cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go flag extraction (line references).\n\n2. **RouteFlags -\u003e RouteRequest normalization** — routing.go:NormalizeRouteRequest. What each CLI flag maps to; which flags are discarded when they don't match an expected shape; the current gap around --provider.\n\n3. **Discovery probe** — discovery.go:DiscoverProviderModels. When it fires; what it caches; the 30s TTL; how failures are handled.\n\n4. **Candidate planning** — routing.go:BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate. For each registered harness, the state map lookup and the viability checks.\n\n5. **Fuzzy matching** — discovery.go:FuzzyMatchModel. Pool selection (global vs per-provider), normalization (current gaps), prefix + tiebreak algorithm.\n\n6. **Scoring** — routing.go:scoreCandidate. Per-profile scoring, cost class, historical success rate, provider-affinity bonus (when implemented).\n\n7. **Candidate ranking** — routing.go:RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate. Non-viable rejection, stable tiebreak.\n\n8. **Dispatch** — runner.go:Run. Harness-specific branches (virtual, agent, script, HTTP-provider-embedded, default exec). For embedded: agent_runner.go:RunAgent -\u003e resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider -\u003e agent library call.\n\n9. **Wire-level request** — ddx-agent builds the HTTP body (system_prompt + messages + tools), sends to the provider. .ddx/executions/20260418T030350-ec108521/prompt.md:5:## Problem

No single document traces the provider/model/harness resolution path from CLI flags to the final HTTP request. SD-015 and SD-023 are SPECS — they describe what should happen in 5 routing modes. The code lives across routing.go, discovery.go, agent_runner.go, runner.go, and agent_cmd.go. A newcomer debugging a routing bug must infer the flow by reading all of them.

The audit for epic ddx-2d974641 turned up a RouteRequest struct missing its Provider field, and a parallel RunOptions.Provider path that bypasses routing — neither of which is documented. This is the kind of hidden structural defect a Resolution Path document would have flagged on first read.

## Fix

Write docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (or similar location; coordinate with SD-015's author). The document traces one concrete invocation end-to-end:

    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6

Walk through every layer, with file:line citations:

1. **CLI parse** — cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go flag extraction (line references).

2. **RouteFlags -> RouteRequest normalization** — routing.go:NormalizeRouteRequest. What each CLI flag maps to; which flags are discarded when they don't match an expected shape; the current gap around --provider.

3. **Discovery probe** — discovery.go:DiscoverProviderModels. When it fires; what it caches; the 30s TTL; how failures are handled.

4. **Candidate planning** — routing.go:BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate. For each registered harness, the state map lookup and the viability checks.

5. **Fuzzy matching** — discovery.go:FuzzyMatchModel. Pool selection (global vs per-provider), normalization (current gaps), prefix + tiebreak algorithm.

6. **Scoring** — routing.go:scoreCandidate. Per-profile scoring, cost class, historical success rate, provider-affinity bonus (when implemented).

7. **Candidate ranking** — routing.go:RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate. Non-viable rejection, stable tiebreak.

8. **Dispatch** — runner.go:Run. Harness-specific branches (virtual, agent, script, HTTP-provider-embedded, default exec). For embedded: agent_runner.go:RunAgent -> resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider -> agent library call.

9. **Wire-level request** — ddx-agent builds the HTTP body (system_prompt + messages + tools), sends to the provider. What the server receives.

10. **Response parsing** — streaming or non-streaming decode; where 'unexpected end of JSON input' (ddx-6a5dfe35) surfaces.

Include a SEQUENCE DIAGRAM (mermaid or ASCII) showing the layers.

Include a GAP TABLE at the top listing every currently-missing piece (as of whatever the SD-015 implementation state is) with links to the specific beads tracking each gap.

Keep the document MAINTAINED alongside the code: update it when RouteRequest gains a field, when a new routing mode lands, when fuzzy match gains a normalization step.

## Files likely touched

- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (new)
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md — add a single-line pointer at the top of the 'Overview' section
- skills/ddx/reference/agents.md — add a one-line pointer in 'Harness / profile / persona dispatch' section so agents consulting the skill can follow up to the full trace when needed

## Out of scope

- Rewriting SD-015 or SD-023 — this doc complements them; they describe intent, this describes current reality.
- Documenting future routing modes that haven't landed.

then picks whichever harness wins on profile — typically claude — which
cannot serve local models. The user must specify --harness lmstudio to
work around it.

The information needed to route correctly already exists: ddx agent check
discovers live LM Studio providers (vidar, bragi) and their advertised
model lists. But BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate never consults
live provider discovery when scoring candidates for a ModelPin. A candidate
with IsHTTPProvider=true whose discovered model list includes the pinned
model should score as viable; candidates that cannot serve the pin should
be rejected.

This is the model→provider discovery gap referenced in ddx-1cfce460. The
PATH fix landed but the discovery wiring did not.

.ddx/executions/20260417T194816-38d98bba/prompt.md:5:Add fuzzy prefix matching to model resolution so --model qwen3.6 resolves
to qwen3.6-35b-a3b when a provider advertises it.

Depends on ddx-71fe194f (discovery integration) being landed first — this
bead extends the unified model pool with prefix matching.

Implementation:
- In the model resolution step, after checking for exact match, check for
  prefix matches across the unified pool (catalog + discovered).
- Tiebreak: shortest suffix after prefix. On equal suffix length, prefer
  catalog entries over discovered-only, then alphabetical.
- When fuzzy match is used, print: model "X" resolved to "Y" (fuzzy match).
- When resolved model has deprecation metadata, print warning.
- When multiple models tie after all tiebreakers, warn and pick first.
.ddx/executions/20260417T194816-38d98bba/prompt.md:8:1. ddx work --model qwen3.6 discovers qwen3.6-35b-a3b via prefix match and routes there.

3. Shortest suffix wins: qwen3.6-27b (suffix len 4) beats qwen3.6-35b-a3b (suffix len 8).
4. On equal suffix length, catalog entry preferred over discovered-only.
5. Fuzzy match prints: model "qwen3.6" resolved to "qwen3.6-27b" (fuzzy match).
6. Deprecated model prints warning but proceeds.

Unit tests:
T1. Prefix match: qwen3.6 → qwen3.6-35b-a3b when only prefix available.
T2. Exact beats prefix: qwen3.6 → qwen3.6 when both exist.
T3. Shortest suffix tiebreak: qwen3.6-27b wins over qwen3.6-35b-a3b.
T4. Catalog preferred over discovered on equal suffix length.
T5. Tie warning emitted when multiple candidates survive all tiebreakers.

.ddx/executions/20260418T063750-16fb3982/prompt.md:5:## Problem

No single document traces the provider/model/harness resolution path from CLI flags to the final HTTP request. SD-015 and SD-023 are SPECS — they describe what should happen in 5 routing modes. The code lives across routing.go, discovery.go, agent_runner.go, runner.go, and agent_cmd.go. A newcomer debugging a routing bug must infer the flow by reading all of them.

The audit for epic ddx-2d974641 turned up a RouteRequest struct missing its Provider field, and a parallel RunOptions.Provider path that bypasses routing — neither of which is documented. This is the kind of hidden structural defect a Resolution Path document would have flagged on first read.

## Fix

Write docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (or similar location; coordinate with SD-015's author). The document traces one concrete invocation end-to-end:

    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6

Walk through every layer, with file:line citations:

1. **CLI parse** — cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go flag extraction (line references).

2. **RouteFlags -> RouteRequest normalization** — routing.go:NormalizeRouteRequest. What each CLI flag maps to; which flags are discarded when they don't match an expected shape; the current gap around --provider.

3. **Discovery probe** — discovery.go:DiscoverProviderModels. When it fires; what it caches; the 30s TTL; how failures are handled.

4. **Candidate planning** — routing.go:BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate. For each registered harness, the state map lookup and the viability checks.

5. **Fuzzy matching** — discovery.go:FuzzyMatchModel. Pool selection (global vs per-provider), normalization (current gaps), prefix + tiebreak algorithm.

6. **Scoring** — routing.go:scoreCandidate. Per-profile scoring, cost class, historical success rate, provider-affinity bonus (when implemented).

7. **Candidate ranking** — routing.go:RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate. Non-viable rejection, stable tiebreak.

8. **Dispatch** — runner.go:Run. Harness-specific branches (virtual, agent, script, HTTP-provider-embedded, default exec). For embedded: agent_runner.go:RunAgent -> resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider -> agent library call.

9. **Wire-level request** — ddx-agent builds the HTTP body (system_prompt + messages + tools), sends to the provider. What the server receives.

10. **Response parsing** — streaming or non-streaming decode; where 'unexpected end of JSON input' (ddx-6a5dfe35) surfaces.

Include a SEQUENCE DIAGRAM (mermaid or ASCII) showing the layers.

Include a GAP TABLE at the top listing every currently-missing piece (as of whatever the SD-015 implementation state is) with links to the specific beads tracking each gap.

Keep the document MAINTAINED alongside the code: update it when RouteRequest gains a field, when a new routing mode lands, when fuzzy match gains a normalization step.

## Files likely touched

- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (new)
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md — add a single-line pointer at the top of the 'Overview' section
- skills/ddx/reference/agents.md — add a one-line pointer in 'Harness / profile / persona dispatch' section so agents consulting the skill can follow up to the full trace when needed

## Out of scope

- Rewriting SD-015 or SD-023 — this doc complements them; they describe intent, this describes current reality.
- Documenting future routing modes that haven't landed.

# ddx-915240dd: routing pickup of agent endpoint-first redesign

## AC checklist (from bead)

1. **DDx config no longer names provider profiles in agent.routing/agent.endpoints.**
   `.ddx/config.yaml` lines 12–27 declare `agent.endpoints` as a list of
   typed `host:port` blocks (lmstudio + omlx). No `vidar-omlx` named
   profile remains.

2. **go.mod tracks the agent release that ships endpoint-first routing.**
   `cli/go.mod:95` pins `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent v0.9.5`,
   which contains the agent-0c6189f5 series:
   `a1ac946 fix: route native providers by live endpoint catalogs`,
   `02ee6bf fix: force live endpoint probe for routing`,
   `7a27837 chore: close endpoint routing review follow-up`.

3. **Drains against cheap-only routing land at live endpoints.**
   - Endpoint probe (`/v1/models`):
     - vidar:1235 (omlx) → 200, lists `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit` etc.
     - bragi:1234 (lmstudio) → 200, lists `qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b` etc.
     - vidar:1234 (lmstudio) → 502 (skipped by `endpointHasLiveModels`).
     - grendel:1234 (lmstudio) → connection timeout (skipped).
   - `ddx agent run --profile cheap --harness agent --text "Reply with the single word: ALIVE"` returned `ALIVE`
     via `provider=omlx-vidar-1235` in 6.3 s
     (input 720 tokens, output 41 tokens). Routing now reaches the live
     omlx endpoint, not a dead `vidar-omlx` named profile.

4. **32 fake-migration children begin making progress on local models.**
   `ddx agent execute-bead ddx-68c372a6 --no-merge --harness agent --context-budget minimal`
   → execution bundle `.ddx/executions/20260422T234723-8b0a7b43/`:
   - `provider`: `omlx-vidar-1235` (live local endpoint)
   - `tokens`: 2,106,268 (2.1 M; 2,093,464 input / 12,804 output)
   - `duration_ms`: 1,991,524 (33 min real agent loop)
   - `status`: `execution_failed` with `reason: iteration_limit`
     — the agent worked the task to its iteration cap, not a 16 ms 404.

   Compare to the pre-pickup baseline recorded on the same bead
   (`manifest.json` events 1–6, 2026-04-21T17:27Z):
   `provider=vidar-omlx model=qwen3.5-27b` → 404 in 16 ms,
   `provider=gemini` smart-tier fallback → cancelled in 24.9 s,
   total cost $0, zero successes across the 30-attempt drain.

   The remaining 31 fake-migration siblings (`ddx-68c372a6` through
   `ddx-27e2b5ce`) share the same routing config and dep on this bead;
   when this bead closes they unblock and `ddx work
   --no-adaptive-min-tier --min-tier cheap --max-tier cheap` will reach
   the same live endpoint.

## Files touched in the prior attempt (a1cd7eb1) that this evidence vouches for

- `.ddx/config.yaml` — endpoint blocks in place
- `cli/go.mod` / `cli/go.sum` — bumped to v0.9.5 (commit 0516888a)
- `cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go` — `serviceConfigFromDDxEndpoints`
  + live-models probe gating registration of dead endpoints
- `cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig_test.go` — httptest live-endpoint
  resolution test
- `cli/internal/config/types.go` — `AgentEndpoint` schema
- `cli/internal/config/schema/config.schema.json` — schema validation
- `cli/internal/config/routing_ladder_test.go` — routing test
